summary: A review of Iris Murdoch's wartime letters and diaries (1939-1945), revealing her early development as a writer and philosopher. The text highlights her intense desire to write, her fluid judgments of character, and her romanticization of emotional peril over conventional security.

principles.statement: Writing is a tool to objectify and examine internal and external psychological conflicts.
principles.rationale: She viewed the novel as a medium to externalize the complex, confusing conflicts she experienced within herself and observed in the people around her.
principles.source_quote: I want to write a long long & exceedingly obscure novel objectifying the queer conflicts I find within myself & observe in the characters of others.

principles.statement: Estimations of character should remain flexible and open to rapid revision.
principles.rationale: Initial impressions of people as conceited or snobbish can quickly be overturned by observing their humility or genuine passions.
principles.source_quote: Strange how quickly one can change estimations of character. He is not the lofty conceited & utterly snobbish young swine I thought he was at all.

mental_models.name: The Sea and Peril vs. Settling Down
mental_models.description: A lens for evaluating life paths and relationships, contrasting the timid, secure, and conventional life ("Settling Down") with the chaotic, dangerous, but intensely alive experience of emotional peril.
mental_models.example: She used this dichotomy to describe her attraction to David Hicks, rejecting a safe marriage in Buckinghamshire for the "madness" and emotional danger he represented.

signature_quotes.text: I want to write a long long & exceedingly obscure novel objectifying the queer conflicts I find within myself & observe in the characters of others.
signature_quotes.context: Expressing her deep ambition to write during a period of exhausting wartime activities.

signature_quotes.text: Strange how quickly one can change estimations of character.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting in her diary on a fellow student she initially misjudged.

signature_quotes.text: After the years of my own timidity & partly living—after the people sober & withdrawn from life—after the various visions of marriage as equals security & Settling Down and a large income and a house in Bucks & no more madness—you are the great wide sea & peril & a high wind.
signature_quotes.context: Writing to David Hicks about her preference for emotional danger over a conventional, safe life.

anti_patterns.description: "Partly living" through a timid pursuit of security and conventionality (e.g., "Settling Down" with a large income and no madness).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It withdraws a person from the intense value, interest, and emotional reality of life, substituting safety for genuine experience.
